stem: Define a map \( \varphi:\mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \to \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) by \( \varphi(x) = [[a]]\,x \bmod [[n]] \) (this is a well-defined group homomorphism since \( [[m]]\cdot[[a]] \equiv 0 \pmod{[[n]]} \)). What is \( \varphi([[x]]) \)?
distractor_rationales: This forgets to reduce modulo [[n]], leaving a number outside \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \).
This multiplies by the domain's modulus [[m]] instead of by [[a]].
This adds instead of multiplying, confusing the homomorphism with an additive shift.
This just restates the input [[x]] without applying \( \varphi \) at all.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( \varphi:\mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \to \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) be the homomorphism \( \varphi(x) = [[a]]\,x \bmod [[n]] \). What is the order of the image \( \varphi(\mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]}) \) (i.e., \( |\operatorname{im}\varphi| \))?
solution: The image is the cyclic subgroup of \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) generated by \( [[a]] = \varphi(1) \), whose order is \( [[n]]/\gcd([[n]],[[a]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( \gcd([[n]],[[a]]) \) itself, not \( n \) divided by it.
This assumes the image is the whole codomain, i.e., that \( \varphi \) is surjective.
This is the order of the DOMAIN, not the image.
This mixes up the two moduli instead of using [[a]], the image of the generator.
This is off by one from the correct image order.
This is off by one from the correct image order.

stem: Let \( \varphi:\mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \to \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) be the homomorphism \( \varphi(x) = [[a]]\,x \bmod [[n]] \). What is the order of the kernel \( \ker\varphi = \{x \in \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} : \varphi(x)=0\} \)?
distractor_rationales: This is the size of the IMAGE, not the kernel.
This assumes \( \varphi \) is the zero map, so the whole domain is the kernel.
This uses the codomain's size instead of computing the kernel via the isomorphism theorem.
This mixes up the two moduli instead of the actual image size.
This is off by one from the correct kernel order.
This is off by one from the correct kernel order.

stem: Consider the sign homomorphism \( \operatorname{sgn}: S_{[[n]]} \to \{1,-1\} \) sending a permutation to \( +1 \) if it is even and \( -1 \) if it is odd. What is the order of \( \ker(\operatorname{sgn}) \) (the alternating group \( A_{[[n]]} \))?
solution: The sign map is surjective onto \( \{1,-1\} \) (order 2), so by the First Isomorphism Theorem \( |\ker(\operatorname{sgn})| = |S_{[[n]]}|/2 = [[n]]!/2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( |S_{[[n]]}| \) itself, forgetting to divide by the image's order 2.
This uses \( ([[n]]-1)! \) instead of \( [[n]]!/2 \).
This divides by 4 instead of 2, as if the image had order 4.
This just restates [[n]] instead of computing \( n!/2 \).
This is off by one from the correct kernel order.
This is off by one from the correct kernel order.
